macro_segments.title: Review of Preceding Debates on Zman in a Get and the Positions of Rabbi Yochanan and Reish Lakish

micro_segments.title: Summary of the Debate Between Rabbi Yochanan and Reish Lakish Regarding When a Get Takes Effect
The Two Major Reasons for Requiring a Zman in a Get: Protecting the Wife from Execution and Protecting the Purchaser of Nichsei Milog
Explanation of Rabbi Shimon's Position on the Predated Get (Get Mukdam) and His Reasoning

macro_segments.title: The Takanah of Chazal Regarding Zman in a Get and Responses to Challenges Against Its Effectiveness

micro_segments.title: The Case of Three Defective Gets Where the Children Are Not Considered Mamzerim Despite the Woman Remarrying
The Concern That the Takanah Is Ineffective If One Can Circumvent It by Writing Without a Zman or by the Husband Removing the Zman
The Response That People Will Investigate and Will Not Accept an Improperly Dated Get, Especially in Cases of International Delivery

macro_segments.title: The Debate Over When the Three-Month Waiting Period Begins: From the Time of Writing or the Time of Delivery

micro_segments.title: The Case of the Two Wives with Different Zmanim Given at the Same Time and Its Resolution
The Position of Rav That the Three-Month Period Begins at the Time of Delivery (Netinah)
The Position of Shmuel That the Three-Month Period Begins at the Time of Writing (Ketivah)
The Final Halachic Ruling That the Three-Month Period Begins at the Time of Writing

macro_segments.title: The Status of a Ketubah Under Shemittah Law and Whether It Qualifies as a Tnai Beis Din Debt

micro_segments.title: Introduction to the Question of Whether a Ketubah Is Cancelled by Shemittah and the Reason It Is Generally Considered a Tnai Beis Din Obligation
The Position of Rav That a Ketubah Becomes Subject to Shemittah Only After Both Commencement of Payment and Explicit Declaration as a Loan
The Position of Shmuel That a Ketubah Becomes Subject to Shemittah After Either Commencement of Payment or Declaration as a Loan
The Baraita Supporting Rav's Position with Explicit Language Requiring Both Conditions
Discussion of Various Categories of Women's Obligations and How They Interact with Shemittah

macro_segments.title: The Parallel Between a Ketubah and Other Acts of Beis Din Regarding the Timing of Writing and Signing

micro_segments.title: Shmuel's Comparison of a Ketubah to a Document of Beis Din, Allowing It to Be Written During the Day and Signed at Night
The Case of Rabbi Chiya bar Rav's Ketubah Written and Signed on Different Days Without Objection from Rav
The Principle That Leniency Applies When the Parties Remain Engaged in the Same Matter

macro_segments.title: Rabbi Shimon's Leniency on Predated Gets and the Scope of His Allowance

micro_segments.title: Rava's Explanation of Rabbi Shimon's Reasoning: Once the Husband Resolves to Divorce, He Cannot Retract (Piyus)
Reish Lakish's Limitation of Rabbi Shimon's Leniency to the Immediate Day and Night, Not Extending to Ten Days
Rabbi Yochanan's Expansion Allowing Up to Ten Days Even According to Rabbi Shimon Due to Public Knowledge of Reconciliation

macro_segments.title: The Case of Giving a Get to Ten People: Whether All Must Sign as Witnesses or Some Sign Due to a Condition

micro_segments.title: The Question of What It Means to Command Ten People to Write a Get and the Distinction Between Those Who Sign as Witnesses and Those Who Sign Due to a Condition
Rabbi Yochanan's Position That Two Sign as Primary Witnesses and the Rest Sign Due to a Condition (Tnai)
Reish Lakish's Position That All Ten Are Witnesses (Edim)
Practical Differences Between the Two Positions Regarding Timing of Signatures and Disqualification

macro_segments.title: The Application of Rabbi Shimon's Lenient Position to the Case of Ten People Signing a Get Over Multiple Days

micro_segments.title: The Case Where Two Signers Sign on One Day and the Remaining Signers Sign Up to Ten Days Later
The Reliance on Rabbi Shimon's Position That a Get Is Valid Even When Written and Signed at Different Times
The Caveat That Rabbi Shimon's Leniency Applies Only in Cases of Extreme Hardship (Sha'at Hadeichak) to Avoid Creating Agunot
The Distinction Between Holding Like Rabbi Shimon L'chatchila Versus Relying on Him in Cases of Emergency
